entry.title: Authorization problem creating Purchasing Requests
entry.content:
Authorization issue: Users are able to create purchase requests in unauthorized purchasing groups. - Example: A user with the role BR_CENTRAL_PURCHASER_ can create purchase requests in purchasing group G40 using document type NB, despite restrictions.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Authorization problem creating Purchasing Requests
entry.content:
Hi,

If multiple catalogs sharing common authorization object to different roles with different restrictions, all these roles are assigned to same user, user will have a union of all these authorizations.

Hence, if Role A allows the authorization and Role B restricts and a user is assigned both Role A and B, then authorization status is allowed. Note that assigning multiple business roles to a business user increases the risk of overriding existing authorizations. 

For detailed information refer to How to Define Authorizations Based on Restrictions

In simple words, if one role restricts authorization and other role has unrestricted access- the authorization is considered as Unrestricted access.

Refer to 3411950 - Restrictions set on a particular field is not working as expected - SAP for Me

Thanks and regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: SAP CAP on HANA XSA: Scaling Industrial Data Applications Beyond the Cloud
entry.content:
In large-scale industrial data processing, handling high volumes of data is not an "edge case"—it is the standard. When thousands or millions of data points from sensors and machinery must be processed in real-time and synchronized via automated jobs, your architecture determines whether the system thrives or grinds to a halt. 

In this article, I will show you how we implemented a Mission-Critical Industrial Data Application using the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) on SAP HANA 2.0 XSA (On-Premise). This combination is rarely discussed, as the CAP framework is primarily known for BTP-based development, but on-premise, it reveals immense power when paired correctly with HANA.

Why Customers Still Rely on On-Premise XSA

While the "Cloud First" strategy is dominant, many customers—especially in industries with critical infrastructure—continue to use SAP HANA XSA On-Premise. Key reasons include:

  • Data Sovereignty & Privacy: Some organizations handle highly sensitive industrial or national data that must remain behind the company firewall for security reasons.

  • Regulatory Compliance: Strict legal requirements in certain sectors prohibit the use of public cloud environments for specific types of operational data.

  • Minimal Latency: In industrial environments, being physically close to the data source (the machines and sensors) minimizes network latency, which is crucial for high-speed processing.

  • Legacy Integration: Direct proximity to existing On-Premise HANA 2.0 landscapes often makes a local XSA deployment more efficient than a hybrid cloud setup.

1. Visualizing the Architecture

To bring it all together, here is the architectural overview of the Industrial Data Platform:

2. The XSA Setup: Exporting CAP as a Module

Unlike in SAP BTP, the XSA environment requires an explicit configuration so the controller can correctly bind the service and manage port assignments. 

The Entry Point: srv/server.js

In an XSA infrastructure, the Node.js module must explicitly export its server handle. While CAP automates much of this in the cloud, on-premise XSA requires this manual step in the server.js:

const cds = require('@sap/cds');

// Optional: Bootstrap for custom middlewares (e.g., OData V2 Proxy)
cds.on('bootstrap', (app) => {
    /* Custom logic, e.g., security headers or logging */
});

// CRITICAL for XSA: Export the server handle.
// This allows the XSA runtime to bind the service to the platform.
module.exports = cds.server;

In the package.json, we reference this via "start": "node srv/server.js".

The Deployment Blueprint: mta.yaml

The mta.yaml is the heart of the infrastructure. To handle parallel loads and optimize deployment, we use specific parameters:

_schema-version: "3.1.0"
ID: idp_platform
parameters:
  enable-parallel-deployments: true # Significantly speeds up the deployment process

modules:
  # --- Backend Module (CAP) ---
  - name: idp_platform-srv
    type: nodejs
    parameters:
      env:
        HDB_NODEJS_THREADPOOL_SIZE: "8" # Critical for parallel DB throughput
    build-parameters:
      commands:
        - npm ci # Guarantees exact versions (Deterministic Build)
        - npx cds build --production
    requires:
      - name: idp_platform-uaa
      - name: idp_platform-hdi

 

3. Evolution: From "Pure Node.js" to SAP CAP

Previously, such industrial solutions were often developed as pure Node.js/Express applications. However, moving to CAP offers massive advantages:

  • Efficiency: CAP provides the OData infrastructure and security (via @(requires: 'Viewer')) automatically.

  • Focus: We concentrate on the Domain Model (CDS), while the framework handles the "plumbing."

  • Hybrid Approach: Local development on SQLite, productive power on HANA.

 

4. The Database Layer: Native Performance with HDI

When millions of records are being ingested, JavaScript object mapping can become a bottleneck. Here, we "drop down" to the database level using specific artifacts in the db folder.

Configuration: .hdiconfig

For HANA to know how to handle our native files, we need an .hdiconfig. It assigns the correct plugins to the file extensions:

{
  "file_extensions": {
    "hdbprocedure": { "plugin_name": "sap.hana.di.procedure" },
    "cds": { "plugin_name": "sap.hana.di.cds" }
  }
}

Native Power: .hdbprocedure

By using a Stored Procedure, we leverage Table-Valued Parameters (TVPs) to pass data packets directly to the HANA core, bypassing framework overhead:

-- Anonymized Procedure for Mass Ingestion
PROCEDURE "proc_handle_mass_ingestion" (
  IN p_timestamps TABLE ( series_ID NVARCHAR(36), seq INTEGER, ts TIMESTAMP ),
  IN p_values TABLE ( series_ID NVARCHAR(36), seq INTEGER, val NVARCHAR(20) )
)
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT SQL SECURITY INVOKER AS
BEGIN
  -- High-efficiency batch insert using SYSUUID
  INSERT INTO "DATA_STORE_TIMESTAMPS" ("ID", "SERIES_ID", "SEQ", "TS")
  SELECT SYSUUID, series_ID, seq, ts FROM :p_timestamps;

  INSERT INTO "DATA_STORE_VALUES" ("ID", "SERIES_ID", "SEQ", "VAL")
  SELECT SYSUUID, series_ID, seq, val FROM :p_values;
END;

 

5. Automation & Real-Time: SAP Job Scheduler

To keep data current, we do not use simple internal app intervals. Instead, we use the SAP Job Scheduler. It triggers our import logic reliably from the outside at defined periodic intervals.

Once the job is completed and the mass data has been processed via the Stored Procedure, real-time updates are triggered in the Angular frontend. By using CAP events or targeted OData refresh triggers in the UI, we ensure that users always see the latest status of industrial assets without needing to reload the page.

6. Conclusion: The Best of Both Worlds

By combining SAP CAP's productivity with the raw performance of HANA Stored Procedures and the SAP Job Scheduler, the IDP platform manages massive data volumes with ease. We use CAP for 90% of the application and native HANA features where performance is the absolute priority.

How are you handling mass data imports in CAP? Ever experienced and/or applied SAP CAP on HANA XSA? I look forward to your comments!

Cheers

Rufat

entry.title: Re: How to move VC elements SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud?
entry.content: In S/4HANA Public Cloud, Customers use the Migration Objects documented at the resource Available Migration Objects under business catalog SAP_LO_BC_VC_MODELING_PC to move the Variant Configuration master data through their landscape

entry.title: Re: SAP TM S4 Hana Public Cloud, How to distribute the co...
entry.content:
Dear @serchbrher,

In Public Cloud TM the Charges Profile / Distribution Profile does not offer distribution by net value / material price. SAP current official training content lists only these four standard distribution profiles for freight cost allocation: gross weight, net weight, gross volume and distance times weight so not seeing a Net Value option in your screenshot is expected standard behawior. SAP also describes the distribution logic as freight cost allocation from the freight order down to the relevant order/delivery items, controlled by the assigned distribution profile and again the documented options are only those four profiles

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-transportation-management/configuring-charge-management-and-explaining-the-charge-calculation-process

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-transportation-management/distributing-freight-costs-2

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Journal Entry Multiple Object Assignment with Statistical Projects
entry.content:
Hello dear SAP Gurus,

   As Cloud Public edition introduced, we know that internal orders are obsolete and no longer available. It means in account assignment block in journal entries with cost elements, a multiple account assignment with ( Internal Order + Cost Center ) or ( Internal order + WBS ) isn't managable, only ( Statistical WBS + Cost Center ) multi-assignment works for overhead costs and this leads to some questions in design of cost flow. 

   Since, our customer in need of tracking for example Vehicle Lease Costs in specific projects, lets assume proj.w/Rev. type WBS X, and wants to track in plate number detail, lets assume stat.proj type WBS Y created accordingly, a specific journal entry needs to be created as follows :

Journal Entry 123 - Actual Post -> WBS X, CElm A -> 100 EUR

Journal Entry 123 - Statistical Post -> WBS Y, CElm A -> 100 EUR

 Because multiple object assignment can not be done by same object type (In this example clearly WBS), we're expecting a solution. The requirement isn't critical and mandatory, yet meaningful and useful. Should we create a custom field to add another attribute or dimension to the journal entry and a CDS view for reporting ? Or should we seperate the statistical use of objects by the source of actual direct costs, for example :

Source of Actual Cost (Main Acc.Asg.) -> Cost Center -> use stat. WBS

Source of Actual Cost (Main Acc.Asg.) -> WBS -> use Cost Center statistical / by cascading assignment

Without detailing this logic for seperate business events and by keeping it simple, how can we achieve the solution with expected result ? 

   Thanks in advance for your guidance.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Finance SAP S/4HANA Cloud for enterprise portfolio and project management

entry.title: Re: SAP HCI - XML to Jason converter cannot handle nesting
entry.content: ML to JSON conversion issues in SAP HCI are quite common especially when nested structures are involved. Make sure your mapping is handling nested nodes properly — sometimes the standard XML to JSON converter in HCI flattens the structure incorrectly. Try using a custom script step in your integration flow to manually parse the nested format. Hope this resolves your issue!

entry.title: Re: SAP HCI - XML to Jason converter cannot handle nesting
entry.content: XML to JSON conversion issues in SAP HCI are quite common especially when nested structures are involved. Make sure your mapping is handling nested nodes properly — sometimes the standard XML to JSON converter in HCI flattens the structure incorrectly. Try using a custom script step in your integration flow to manually parse the nested format. Hope this resolves your issue.

entry.title: Custom Fields in SAP Service and Asset Manager (SSAM) consuming backend data
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I am currently working on enhancements in SAP Service and Asset Manager (SSAM) using SAP Mobile Development Kit (MDK) and I need to add custom fields during the Notification creation process.

These fields already exist in the SAP PM backend, but I am not fully sure about the correct end-to-end architecture required for SSAM to consume and persist these fields.

My main questions are related to the correct flow between backend, OData service, metadata and SSAM configuration.

What I would like to understand is:

Backend requirement
If a new field is required for Notifications, does it need to be implemented in ABAP (for example via append structures or enhancements) and exposed through the OData service used by SSAM?

OData exposure
Once the field exists in the backend, is it mandatory to extend the OData service entity to include the new field so that it becomes available in the MDK metadata?

MDK metadata and application layer
After the field is available in the OData metadata, should it then be added in the MDK project by modifying:

Pages

FormCell controls

Create/Update actions

ConfigPanel usage
Some SSAM fields appear to be controlled via the ConfigPanel.

My question is:
Can the ConfigPanel be used to create and persist completely new custom fields, or is it only used to enable/disable fields that already exist in the metadata and application?

Entities in SSAM
If a field is not part of the current OData entity used by Notifications, is it necessary to:

extend the backend entity

regenerate metadata

and then bind the field in the MDK UI?

I would appreciate if someone could confirm the recommended approach for implementing custom fields in SSAM that interact with the backend.

Any documentation or best practices would also be very helpful.

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: SAP DM and co11n parallel
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

I would appreciate your clarification on whether SAP Digital Manufacturing (SAP DM) and CO11N in S/4HANA can be used in parallel for production confirmations.

In our current setup, certain critical machines provide real-time data through PLC and DCS integration. For these machines, we intend to capture production confirmations through SAP DM. However, some downstream machines, such as those used in later stages (e.g., packing and slitting), are less prone to downtime and do not currently provide data through PLC/DCS.

Given this scenario, I would like to understand if it is feasible to confirm quantities for specific work centers within a production order via SAP DM, while confirming the remaining operations of the same order manually through the CO11N transaction in S/4HANA.

Your guidance on whether this hybrid confirmation approach is technically supported and advisable would be greatly appreciated.

Best regards,

SAP Digital Manufacturing SAP Digital Manufacturing Insights

entry.title: ASP.NET Crystal Report Viewer failing to display a report due to javascript error
entry.content:
Hello,

Environment:

  • Crystal Reports Runtime SP39
  • .NET Framework 4.8
  • Using WebForms with CrystalReportViewer
  • CrystalReportViewer13

I've noticed a javascript console error happening on a specific report that I am trying to preview, causing the CrystalReportViewer to fail to render the report. Exporting the report works fine. Previewing the report using a non web-based CrystalReportViewer is also working fine.  The report is creating Sections with values from the database and I've narrowed down the error to be caused by a value like "<A>C</A>" being passed to the javascript that is causing the following error:

Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'addEventListener')
    at addDblClickCB (allInOne.js:32:37791)
    at Object.TreeWidgetElem_init [as initOld] (allInOne.js:32:147224)
    at Object.init (allInOne.js:34:48433)

entry.title: Somos SAP Community Curitiba – Simone Azevedo
entry.content:
Este post faz parte da série “Somos SAP Community Curitiba”, criada para compartilhar as histórias das pessoas que ajudam a construir e fortalecer o ecossistema SAP na nossa cidade.


A ideia da série foi apresentada neste post de abertura:
➡️Introdução - Série da comunidade: Somos SAP Community Curitiba

A proposta é simples: responder algumas perguntas e contar um pouco da nossa jornada dentro da comunidade.


👩‍💻Quem sou eu na SAP Community Curitiba?
Sou Simone Azevedo, consultora SAP e curadora da SAP Community Curitiba.
Meu papel na comunidade é ajudar a conectar pessoas, promover eventos e incentivar a troca de conhecimento entre profissionais do ecossistema SAP.
Acredito muito que comunidades são espaços poderosos para aprendizado, networking e crescimento profissional.


💙O que mais me motiva a continuar fazendo parte da SAP Community Curitiba?
O que mais me motiva são as pessoas.
Cada evento, cada encontro e cada conversa mostram que quando profissionais se reúnem para compartilhar conhecimento, todos crescem juntos.
Ver alguém sair inspirado de um evento, criar novas conexões ou descobrir novos caminhos na carreira é algo que realmente faz valer a pena.


🚀Como fazer parte da comunidade impactou minha jornada profissional ou pessoal?
A comunidade ampliou muito minha visão sobre o ecossistema SAP.
Além do crescimento profissional, ela me proporcionou algo ainda mais especial: amizades, parcerias e um verdadeiro senso de pertencimento.
A SAP Community Curitiba me ensinou que tecnologia conecta sistemas — mas comunidades conectam pessoas.


📸Um momento especial na comunidade


SAP Inside Track 2019 - 1ª Edição em Curitiba
Essa foto registra um momento muito especial da minha jornada na comunidade: a organização do primeiro SAP Inside Track em Curitiba.  Na foto, um time incrível de voluntários que ajudou a transformar esse evento em realidade. Muito obrigada! 💙
Meu agradecimento também ao @faustomotter (segunda foto) , pelo apoio e incentivo desde o início, e ao @Marssel700  , que mesmo atuando remotamente foi parte importante dessa construção.


🌐Conecte-se comigo
LinkedIn:
👉https://www.linkedin.com/in/simone-azevedo⁠


✨Convidados para continuar a série
Para continuar a série Somos SAP Community Curitiba, convido: @LuizSouza , @AdrianoCWB , @Marssel700 ,  @Raquelamar , @Fabiele 
para compartilharem suas histórias e experiências na comunidade.


Além das perguntas da série:
1️⃣Quem sou eu na SAP Community Curitiba?
2️⃣O que mais me motiva a continuar fazendo parte da SAP Community Curitiba?
3️⃣Como fazer parte da comunidade impactou minha jornada profissional ou pessoal?


Deixo também uma pergunta adicional:
❓Que conselho você daria para alguém que está começando agora no ecossistema SAP e ainda não conhece a comunidade?


✨Frase final
A SAP Community Curitiba é feita por pessoas que acreditam no poder de compartilhar conhecimento, conectar profissionais e construir oportunidades juntos.
Cada história compartilhada fortalece ainda mais essa comunidade que tanto nos inspira.

#SomosSAPCommunityCWB
#SAPCommunityCWB
#SAPCommunity

entry.title: Error calling method "cl_apj_rt_api=>schedule_job" from RAP App
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I've been dealing with an issue when calling method "cl_apj_rt_api=>schedule_job" from the behavior implementation class of a custom RAP App.

The requirement is as follows:

  • The user will select a register from the report.
  • Based on the selected register, the program should print (in a physical printer) a PDF generated from an Adobe Forms. 

To do this, I've added an action in the Behavior Definition. This action (named 'Print') is called when the user selects a register and clicks a custom button.

In the implementation of action 'Print', we're trying to create a Job that will handle the calls to the necessary functions to generate and print the PDF.

METHODS print FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION label~print.

METHOD print.
lv_job_template_name = 'ZJT_MYJOB'.
ls_job_start_info-start_immediately = abap_true.
cl_apj_rt_api=>schedule_job(  EXPORTING    iv_job_template_name = lv_job_template_name
                                                     iv_job_text = 'SPOOL Registration Job'
                                                     is_start_info = ls_job_start_info ).
ENDMETHOD.

To create the Job, we've already created an Application Job Template, the corresponding Application Job Catalog Entry, and the implementing class.

However, when the app is executed, we are getting an error at the time method "schedule_job" is executed:

 I understand that the 'Insert' statement is causing the issue, but is there any way to work around this error?

entry.title: LeanIX + SAP Signavio: Connecting Your Application Portfolio to Your Business Processes
entry.content:
In my previous blog Here  " Integrated Toolchain: LeanIX + SAP Cloud ALM: Auto-Discovering Your SAP Landscape" , I covered the LeanIX + Cloud ALM integration ,how connecting those two tools gives Enterprise Architects an automatically maintained, always-accurate view of their SAP landscape. That's the foundation. Now we build on it. This Blog covers the integration between LeanIX and SAP Signavio what it does when your application portfolio and your business processes finally talk to each other.

 

The Gap Between IT and the Business

Most organisations running SAP have both an enterprise architecture function and a business process management function. They work in parallel . EAs managing the application landscape in LeanIX, BPM teams modelling and improving processes in Signavio. Both doing important work. Both operating largely in isolation from each other.

The consequence shows up at the worst moments. An application is flagged for decommissioning  and nobody has mapped which business processes depend on it. A process is redesigned and IT doesn't know which systems will need to change. A capability gap is identified and there's no clear line between the capability, the processes that execute it, and the applications that support those processes.

You can't make good architectural decisions without understanding the processes the architecture supports. And you can't redesign processes effectively without knowing what the applications underneath them actually do.

The LeanIX–Signavio integration closes this gap. It creates a live, bi-directional connection between the application portfolio and the process model , so that both EA and BPM teams are always working from the same picture.

What the Integration Does

The LeanIX–Signavio integration is an out-of-the-box connection that requires no coding to activate. Once live, it creates a continuous, automated sync between the two platforms  enriching both simultaneously.

 [ Diagram: LeanIX + SAP Signavio — bidirectional integration flow ]

For a deeper look at how this integration plays out across real transformation scenarios, check these 10 Use-cases of Signavio and LeanIX Integration blog on SAP Community by @JonasGermann Link here

From Signavio into LeanIX

From Signavio into LeanIX  once the integration is configured and synchronisation rules are mapped, Process Fact Sheets in LeanIX are automatically created from the processes selected for synchronisation in Signavio. When a process is documented in Signavio, the corresponding fact sheet appears in LeanIX without manual effort with BPMN diagrams remaining in Signavio and linked directly from the fact sheet.  Process documentation updates in Signavio  including process name, hierarchy, and metadata  are automatically reflected in the LeanIX Process Fact Sheet. Additionally, application-to-process relationships can be mapped so that LeanIX links each process to its supporting Application Fact Sheets. The result: LeanIX consistently reflects the current process landscape and its application dependencies as changes occur in Signavio

From any LeanIX Process Fact Sheet, you can jump directly to the corresponding Signavio process model. The connection is live — one click, no searching.

From LeanIX into Signavio

From LeanIX into Signavio  Applications captured in LeanIX can be linked to processes in Signavio and used directly in process modelling. In the Signavio Dictionary, mapped LeanIX data such as Applications, Business Capabilities, and Data Objects  is reflected automatically based on the integration configuration settings. When a process modeller in Signavio clicks on a system in a process diagram, they can jump directly to the corresponding LeanIX Application Fact Sheet.

This means Business Process Managers are no longer modelling in a vacuum. Once relationships are mapped, they can see exactly which IT systems support each step of a process  and Enterprise Architects can see exactly which processes are affected when an application changes, with reporting from both systems displaying the full relationship between Processes and Applications.

 

What flows between LeanIX and Signavio

• Signavio → LeanIX: Process Fact Sheets created automatically from Signavio processes selected for synchronisation

• Signavio → LeanIX: Process documentation updates reflected automatically in LeanIX fact sheets

• LeanIX → Signavio: Applications, Business Capabilities, and Data Objects available in the Signavio Dictionary  based on integration configuration settings

• LeanIX → Signavio: Applications can be linked to process steps directly within Signavio process models

• Bidirectional navigation: jump from a LeanIX Process Fact Sheet to Signavio, and from a Signavio IT system to LeanIX  with one click

• Reporting: relationships between Processes and Applications visible across both platforms

 

Why It Matters

This integration changes the quality of decisions on both sides of the EA–BPM boundary.

For Enterprise Architects

Application portfolio decisions are no longer made in isolation from the processes the portfolio supports. When an EA team evaluates an application for rationalization, decommissioning, or modernization, they can see which business processes that application supports and how extensively. The risk of decommissioning a system that underpins a critical process becomes visible before a decision is made, not after.
The integration also enables a more precise architecture analysis. By combining process insights and value potentials from Signavio with the application portfolio data in LeanIX, EAs can identify where IT infrastructure is over- or under-serving the business — and prioritize investment accordingly.

For Business Process Managers

Process modelling in Signavio becomes grounded in architectural reality. Rather than referencing systems by name or from memory, BPM teams can pull actual LeanIX Applications directly into their process diagrams  with the application's LeanIX profile accessible directly from the process diagram. Process designs reflect what IT actually provides, not what someone assumed it provides.
When process redesign is needed as part of a move to SAP standard processes for S/4HANA, for example  BPM teams can see which applications are involved and what the IT impact of each design decision will be, provided application-to-process relationships have been mapped. Standardized process modelling is supported through the automated import of Applications and other objects from LeanIX.

For the Transformation Program

One of the most underappreciated capabilities this integration unlocks is impact analysis across silos. When a system change is planned, the integrated view shows which processes that system supports. When a process is redesigned, the integrated view shows which applications are involved. Instead of running separate IT and business workstreams that only connect at governance checkpoints, the two streams share a continuously updated data layer throughout the program.

 

Case Study: Decommissioning Without Blind Spots

Consider a utilities company preparing to decommission a legacy ERP module as part of their S/4HANA migration. Without the LeanIX–Signavio integration, this kind of dependency mapping typically happens through stakeholder interviews and manual discovery ,weeks into the program, after planning has already begun.

With this integration active, the EA team can see immediately which active process models in Signavio reference that module across every business unit before a single workshop has been scheduled. Dependencies surface on day one of the architecture review, not after the migration sequence has been locked. Scope changes that would have cost weeks of rework become preventable decisions instead of reactive ones.

This is the shift the integration enables: from discovering what a system touches after a decision are made, to knowing it before one is.

Who Benefits

This integration enriches the experience for two distinct user groups simultaneously — which is what makes it one of the most strategically valuable connections in the toolchain.

  • Enterprise Architects and Application Owners: gain process context for every application — seeing which processes each application supports, how extensively, and what the impact of any change will be
  • Business Process Managers: gain architectural grounding for every process — seeing which IT systems support each process step, with live links to the full application profile in LeanIX
  • Transformation Program Managers: gain a unified view that spans IT and business workstreams — enabling cross-silo impact analysis that was previously impossible without manual coordination
  • S/4HANA Migration Teams: can validate process coverage against the target application landscape before go-live, identifying gaps and conflicts before they become defects

Ready to Activate It?

Here are 3 resources to help you set up the integration. It is straightforward and if you have admin rights in SAP LeanIX, it won't take long:

  • SAP Signavio Integration — SAP LeanIX Documentation → [Documentation]
  • Integrating SAP LeanIX and SAP Signavio → [Learning Journey]
  • For detailed steps on integration and 10 real-world use cases — check the SAP Community blog by Jonas → [From Process to Architecture: 10 Use Cases of SAP Signavio & LeanIX Integration]

The integration is activated through the LeanIX Administration area under Integrations  no coding required. The setup involves configuring authentication against SAP Signavio, mapping processes from Signavio to LeanIX, mapping business context from LeanIX to Signavio, and setting the synchronisation schedule. Multi-tenancy is supported — you can connect multiple Signavio instances with separate configurations and scheduling.

Before enabling the integration, ensure you have fundamental knowledge of Signavio BPM, SAP LeanIX data model best practices, and the ability to set up integration user credentials and permissions within your instance.

 

Setup & Learning Resources

• SAP Help Portal :SAP Signavio Integration with LeanIX: help.sap.com/docs/leanix/ea/sap-signavio-integration

• LeanIX Academy : SAP Signavio Integration course: academy.leanix.net/sap-signavio-integration

•  LeanIX Product Page  SAP Signavio Integration overview: leanix.net/en/products/enterprise-architecture-management/integrations/sap-signavio-integration

•   SAP Whitepaper — Integrating Enterprise Architecture Management and Business Process Transformation: leanix.net/en/resources/download

•  SAP Community — From Process to Architecture: 10 Use Cases of SAP Signavio & LeanIX Integration: community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/from-process-to-architecture-10-use-cases-of-sap-signavio-amp-leanix/ba-p/14335897

Finally, have you connected LeanIX and SAP Signavio in your organisation? Share your success story in the comments — I'd love to hear from you.

 

Next: Blog 3 — SAP Signavio + SAP Cloud ALM

Blog 2 connected the application portfolio to the process model. Blog 3 takes the next step in the Digital Thread: connecting those process models to implementation and delivery through the Signavio–Cloud ALM integration. Blog 3 publishes tomorrow.

 

Ramy Salem

ANZ Toolchain Ambassador at SAP

PhD Candidate – AI Agents for Enterprise Architecture

Certified in SAP | WalkMe | LeanIX | Signavio   ·   TOGAF Certified

Read the Toolchain Blogs that inspired this series:

  • Why the SAP Integrated Toolchain Is the Future of Digital Transformation
  • Integrated Toolchain: LeanIX + SAP Cloud ALM: Auto-Discovering Your SAP Landscape 
  • How Enterprise Architects Can Unlock Strategic Value with WalkMe in SAP Landscapes
  • How Enterprise Architects Can Unlock Strategic Value with SAP Signavio in SAP Landscapes
  • Bridging Strategy to Execution: How LeanIX, Signavio, and WalkMe Create the Ultimate EA Toolchain

entry.title: Hello SAP Community
entry.content:
Hi everyone! I’m Enzo from the Philippines.

I’m currently undergoing training on SAP PaPM Universal Model, and so far, it has been both challenging and exciting. SAP definitely has a steep learning curve, but that’s exactly what makes the journey rewarding—I’m constantly learning something new.

A bit about me: I graduated in October 2025 with a degree in Computer Science. My first professional experience was as a Quality Assurance Specialist Intern, where I focused mostly on software testing. I’m now working as a Quality Assurance Engineer (L1) in a different company. I honestly feel privileged (and a bit surprised!) to be working with SAP systems this early in my career. Although the concepts and processes can be overwhelming at times, I see it as a great opportunity to grow and expand my skills. I'm excited throughout my training as I'll gradually learn on how to use and apply SAP PaPM in real-world financial decision-making and scenarios. 

Looking forward to learning, sharing insights, and connecting with the SAP community. Nice to meet you all!

entry.title: From Sales Data to Design Blueprints: How RPT-1 Enabled Our Fashion Inverse Design Engine
entry.content:
What We Built

The Fashion Trend Alchemist is a fully functional inverse design engine that turns historical sales data into concrete design recommendations — complete with product visualizations, a catchy product name, and compelling sales copy. It works across product categories: coats, t-shirts, dresses, trousers, even shoes and sunglasses. The system adapts to any product type without retraining or reconfiguration.
 
How it creates a product — the full pipeline:
The product creation workflow: from context selection through AI enrichment and RPT-1 prediction to the final product profile with images, name, and sales text. The refine loop lets users iterate on any design decision.
As shown in the diagram above, product creation flows through four core phases:
1. Context Setup — Select a product category (e.g., men's winter coats), define filters and date ranges, and let the system build a curated context of top and bottom performers from the sales data
2. Enrichment — A Vision LLM analyzes product images to extract detailed design attributes (collar type, button style, fabric weight...) that don't exist in the raw database
3. Prediction — The user specifies which attributes to lock and which to predict; RPT-1 fills in the optimal values using in-context learning from the enriched dataset
4. Product Profile — The system generates multi-view product images, proposes a product name, and drafts sales copy — creating a complete, tangible design concept that can be refined and saved to a collection
 
Technical Architecture
This isn't a Jupyter notebook experiment — it's a deployed, interactive application where users explore design decisions in real time.
 
The Fashion Trend Alchemist runs on SAP BTP's Kyma runtime as a three-container deployment. The React frontend communicates with a Fastify/Node.js backend that orchestrates three AI services: RPT-1 for attribute prediction and GPT-4.1 for data enrichment and text generation (both via SAP AI Core), and a **self-hosted Z-Image Turbo** instance (product visualization, deployed as a Kyma microservice). PostgreSQL, Redis, and SeaweedFS handle data persistence, caching, and image storage respectively.
 
How It Works: Designing a Men's Winter Coat
Let's walk through a concrete example to see each phase in action: a product manager wants to know what the next successful men's winter coat should look like.
 
Building the Context
The user selects "Coats" as the product type, filters for winter seasons and men's wear, and the system queries a large transaction dataset for matching products.
For each matching product, the system calculates a velocity score — first computing units sold per day of availability (transaction count divided by days between first and last sale), then converting this to a percentile rank (0-100) across all matching products. A coat that sold 200 units in 30 days scores higher than one that sold 200 units over 6 months. The system then selects the top and bottom performers to create a balanced context showing RPT-1 clear examples of "success" and "failure."
 
Enriching Products with Design Attributes
Fashion databases store generic columns: color, material, product group. But a coat's success depends on collar type, insulation level, and closure style — attributes that don't exist in the raw data.

We solve this with AI enrichment. First, an LLM generates a product-specific attribute schema (e.g., `collar_type`, `button_style`, `insulation_level` with their possible values). Then, GPT-4.1 analyzes each product's image and extracts these attributes as structured data.
Real-time enrichment with progress tracking — GPT-4.1 extracts structured design attributes from product images.
 
RPT-1 in Action: The Prediction
Now the core moment. The enriched context — ~50 products with their detailed attributes and velocity scores — is sent to RPT-1. The user constructs a query row where some attributes are fixed and others contain `[PREDICT]` placeholders.
 
 
The interface uses a three-column layout:

- Locked (left): Attributes the user wants to fix — "I know I want a Navy coat"
- AI-Predicted (center): Attributes RPT-1 should determine — "What collar type? What button style?"
- Excluded (right): Attributes irrelevant to this prediction
 
The user sets a target success score and clicks "Transmute." RPT-1 receives the full context plus the query row in a single API call — no model training, no feature engineering, no pipeline orchestration. Formatted tabular data in, predictions out.

The entire ML layer is essentially: _format data as rows → add query row with `[PREDICT]` → call API → parse response_. The system complexity lives in data preparation, not model management.
 
Bringing It to Life: The Full Product Profile
The predicted attributes are combined with locked values to generate a complete product profile. The system translates the attribute combination into structured image prompts and produces multiple product views via a self-hosted image generation model running on Kyma — using category-aware prompt templates (coats use ghost mannequin photography; footwear uses product pair shots; accessories get contextual framing). This is completed by a catchy product name and a sales text.
 
The result isn't an abstract list of attributes — it's a concrete product concept that a designer or product manager can evaluate, iterate on, and refine. The refine loop shown in the pipeline diagram lets users adjust any attribute and regenerate until they're satisfied, then save the design to a collection.
 
What We Learned About RPT-1
Prediction Becomes a Data Formatting Problem
RPT-1 is pretrained and uses in-context learning — you don't train the model, you provide examples and a query. The API structure is remarkably simple:
The entire prediction integration is ~50 lines of TypeScript. No training scripts, no model serialization, no serving infrastructure. This drastically lowers the barrier for teams outside ML/data science — if you can format tabular data, you can use RPT-1.

Dynamic schemas come free: coats need `collar_type` and `button_style`, sunglasses need `lens_tint` and `frame_shape`. With traditional ML, changing features means re-encoding, retraining, revalidating. With RPT-1, just change the columns.
 
Validating RPT-1 in the Fashion Domain
Our goal wasn't to build a production prediction system — it was to test whether RPT-1's in-context learning could handle fashion data meaningfully. To validate this, we inverted the workflow: instead of predicting attributes from success scores (inverse design), we tested how well RPT-1 predicts success from attributes (forward prediction). This serves as a sanity check — if the model understands attribute-success relationships, it can leverage them for inverse predictions.

We benchmarked RPT-1 against traditional gradient boosting models (XGBoost, CatBoost) across five product categories using an 80/20 random split:
Model
R² 
NDCG 
Hit@25
RPT-1
+0.302
0.954
60.5%
XGBoost
+0.267
0.946
55.1%
CatBoost
+0.258
0.945
 56.4%
Aggregated across five product categories. RPT-1 matches established gradient boosting models on absolute fit (R²) and edges them out on ranking quality (NDCG, Hit@25) — the metrics that matter most for separating winners from losers.
 
The scatter plot below shows this concretely for ladies' dresses: top performers (Q4, green) cluster where predicted, while bottom performers (Q1, red) separate clearly. The model isn't perfect, but the quartile separation confirms RPT-1 successfully learned from the in-context examples.
Actual vs. predicted rank for Ladies All-Season Dresses (ρ = 0.626). Clear quartile separation demonstrates RPT-1 effectively distinguishes high from low performers using only in-context examples.
 
The key finding: RPT-1 worked out-of-the-box for fashion data without domain-specific tuning. For teams exploring tabular foundation models in new domains, this suggests the pretrained capabilities transfer broadly. RPT-1's in-context learning architecture particularly shines in low-data scenarios — some niche categories had only ~23 items in the context, where RPT-1 handled these gracefully.
 
 
 
 
Context Quality Matters More Than Context Structure
We systematically tested what affects RPT-1's prediction quality:

Row and column ordering — virtually no measurable impact. Shuffling the context rows or reordering columns didn't change results.

Column naming — slight but inconsistent effect. Renaming attributes to synonyms, German translations, or even numeric codes showed minor variations, but no clear pattern.
Column naming — slight but inconsistent effect. Renaming attributes to synonyms, German translations, or even numeric codes showed minor variations, but no clear pattern.
 
Out-of-distribution contamination — high impact. When wrong products polluted the context (e.g., shirts mixed into a hoodies dataset), prediction quality degraded significantly.
 
Spearman correlation drops as OOD contamination increases from 0% to 40%. Clean data matters far more than clever formatting.

The takeaway: focus more on data cleaning and proper normalization, rather than perfecting the data structure.
 
Challenges We Faced and How We Solved Them
Real-World Data is Messy

Problem: Product catalogs contain labeling errors — shirts categorized as hoodies, skirts labeled as pants. In-context learning amplifies these errors: garbage in, garbage out.

Solution: Our Vision LLM enrichment returns a `mismatchConfidence` score (0-100) alongside extracted attributes. Products scoring above 80 are flagged for human review. The user sees a prefiltered list and can correct or exclude problematic items before prediction. What started as an afterthought became essential.

GenAI Output is Unpredictable

Problem: LLM outputs can be wrong or malformed — invalid JSON, attributes outside valid ranges, unexpected structures.

Solution: Multi-layer guardrails:

- Prompting with positive examples and explicit output structure
- Runtime validation with Zod schemas to catch structural errors
- Attribute values validated against the ontology's allowed options
- Retry logic with exponential backoff (up to 3 attempts)
 
Users can manually adjust results or send them back to the LLM with feedback.
 
Consistency Across Generated Images
Problem: Three product views (front, back, model) must show the same design. Different prompts lead to inconsistent products.

Solution: The LLM generates a single shared `productDescription` used as the consistent core across all views, combined with view-specific prefixes and category-aware templates (wearables use ghost mannequin photography; footwear uses product pair shots).
 
Closing

Three takeaways for your next project:

1. RPT-1 turns prediction into a data selection & formatting problem. No training pipeline, no ML expertise required — just clean tabular data with a success metric.

2. Context quality beats context structure. Row ordering and naming conventions barely matter; wrong data in the context matters enormously.

3. Build guardrails around GenAI. Validate LLM outputs with schemas, constrain responses to allowed values, and keep humans in the loop for edge cases.
 
 
Developed at BTP Solution Advisory APAC by Danylo Polishchuk and Nils Dittrich.
 
For questions or collaboration opportunities, connect with us on LinkedIn: Danylo Polishchuk | Nils Dittrich.
 
Want to see the Fashion Trend Alchemist in action? Check out the demo video for a walkthrough of the complete workflow.

Want to try RPT-1 yourself? Explore the RPT-1 Playground to test tabular predictions with your own data.

entry.title: 通过 SAP S/4HANA Cloud 中的公司代码限制增强成本中心安全性
entry.content:
简介

安全和访问控制是ERP系统的基本支柱。随着组织不断发展且数据越来越敏感，对精细权限控制的需求变得至关重要。SAP S/4HANA Cloud 继续发展其权限框架，以满足这些关键业务需求。例如: Authorization on company code level for app Manage Cost Centers F1443A

权限体系结构的一个重要增强是引入基于公司代码的公司代码限制。此新功能代表向前迈出的重要一步，有助于组织更严格地控制可以跨不同业务实体访问和管理成本中心信息的人员。

在此博客中，我们将探索此权限增强、其对系统管理员的影响以及确保此功能在 SAP S/4HANA Cloud 即将发布的版本 (2608) 中处于激活状态时实现平稳转换所需的步骤。

场景：了解业务需求

成本中心访问控制的挑战

成本中心是用于在不同部门和业务单位之间分配成本、跟踪费用以及管理财务报表的关键组织结构。在具有多个公司代码的大型组织（代表不同的法律实体或业务部门）中，管理谁可以访问哪些成本中心变得越来越复杂。

考虑以下真实场景：

一家全球制造公司经营多个子公司，每个子公司都有自己的公司代码：

- 公司代码 1000（欧洲分部）

- 公司代码 2000（亚洲分部）

- 公司代码 3000（美洲分部）

每个部门都有自己的成本中心和财务经理。之前，在没有详细的公司代码限制的情况下，有权进行成本中心管理的用户可能会无意中访问所有公司代码的成本中心数据 - 可能会暴露敏感财务信息并产生合规性风险。

解决方案：为成本中心部署公司代码限制

新的基于公司代码的成本中心受限访问功能使管理员能够：

1.限制成本中心可见性：限制用户仅查看与其授权公司代码相关联的成本中心

2.控制修改权限：确保用户只能修改其已分配公司代码中的成本中心主数据

3.加强合规性：满足数据分离和访问控制方面的法规要求

4.改进审计跟踪：创建明确、可审计的记录，提供谁访问哪些成本中心的信息

实施详细信息

  1. 权限要求

要配置和管理公司代码限制，管理员必须具有特定权限：

所需业务目录：

- `SAP_CORE_BC_IAM_RM` - 此业务目录必须分配给负责身份和访问管理 (IAM) 的管理员

这可确保只有合格的 IAM 专业人员才能修改限制配置并防止对安全设置进行未经授权的更改。

 

  1.  配置步骤

管理员必须采取主动步骤来配置公司代码限制，然后才能成为强制限制。方法如下：

关键配置工具：

- 应用：维护业务角色 (F1492)

- 操作：为管理成本中心主数据的所有业务角色预配置公司代码限制值

配置流程：

1.打开维护业务角色应用 (F1492)

2.选择需要公司代码限制的业务角色

3.导航到限制配置部分

4.为限制分配相应的公司代码值

  1. 保存并激活配置

最佳实践：请在系统的低使用期间执行此配置，并在应用于生产系统之前在非生产环境中全面测试。

  1. 受影响的技术对象

公司代码限制将影响 SAP 系统多个层的访问控制：

CDS 视图（核心数据服务）：

- `I_CostCenter` – 主要成本中心主数据视图

- `I_CostCenterVH` – 成本中心值帮助视图

- `I_CostCenterSTDVH` – 成本中心标准值帮助

- `I_CostCenterTP_2` – 成本中心交易过账视图

数据字典值帮助：

- `KOST` – 成本中心的传统值帮助

业务对象接口：

- `I_CostCenterTP_2` – 业务对象处理成本中心事务

  1. 系统行为的预期更改

公司代码限制生效后，将发生以下更改：

访问限制：

- 不具备适当公司代码权限的用户将无法再查看已分配公司代码之外的成本中心主数据

- 尝试访问未经授权的成本中心将导致数据不可用

- 涉及成本中心选择的系统操作将根据公司代码权限过滤选项

对用户体验的影响：

- 值帮助下拉列表（F4 帮助）将仅显示已授权的成本中心

- 报表和分析将仅显示与已分配公司代码相关的数据

- 成本中心搜索将返回过滤结果

管理影响：

- 管理员必须确保所有受影响的业务角色都已配置公司代码限制

- 配置限制失败可能导致用户对之前可用数据的访问权限丢失

 配置时间表和建议

 第1阶段：立即操作（当前）

- 检查成本中心管理的当前权限设置

- 识别处理成本中心主数据的所有业务角色

— 审计当前用户访问模式以建立基本数据

 

 第 2 阶段：预配置（升级前）

- 使用维护业务角色应用 (F1492) 配置公司代码限制

- 为每个业务角色分配相应的公司代码值

- 开发/沙盒环境中的测试配置

 

 第 3 阶段：验证和测试

- 与每个公司代码的代表用户执行端到端测试

- 验证用户是否只能访问授权的成本中心

- 测试值帮助、报表和事务访问

— 记录任何权限访问的问题或意外行为

 

 第 4 阶段：部署（发布时）

- 将更新部署到生产系统

- 监控系统性能和用户反馈

- 及时解决任何访问问题

- 维护变更文档

 

有关此增强的附加信息：

- SAP 注释3632368 - Restricted Access to Cost Centers Based on Company Code - SAP for Me

- SAP 文档: Restricted Access to Cost Centers Based on Company Code | SAP Help Portal

 结论

成本中心主数据管理中引入公司代码限制是 SAP S/4HANA Cloud 权限功能的很有意义进步。使组织能够基于公司代码分配实施精细访问控制，此功能可以增强安全性、改进合规性并降低意外数据暴露的风险。

 

关键要点：

1.主动操作至关重要：不要等到配置公司代码限制时必须使用此功能。现在使用维护业务角色应用预配置值。

2.需要全面计划：审计当前权限结构，识别受影响的业务角色，并提前计划配置策略。

3.测试至关重要：在部署到生产之前彻底测试非生产环境中的配置，以防止用户访问问题。

4.管理监督：确保具有相应权限 (SAP_CORE_BC_IAM_RM) 的管理员管理这些配置以维护安全性。

5.持续合规：随着组织结构和用户角色的发展，定期审核和维护公司代码限制。

对于致力于保持强大的安全控制和法规合规性的组织，此增强完全符合身份和访问管理的最佳实践。通过采用结构化、计划良好的实施方法，管理员可以在即将到来的 SAP S/4HANA Cloud 版本中强制实施公司代码限制时确保无缝过渡。

 

现在是行动的时候了。立即开始预配置工作，以确保此关键权限增强生效时的业务连续性和安全性。

后续步骤：

- 计划检查当前成本中心权限结构

- 识别需要公司代码限制配置的业务角色

- 规划实施时间表，与即将进行的系统更新保持一致

- 联系 SAP 系统管理员，开始预配置工作

entry.title: Manage Purchase Contracts App – Output Details Greyed Out in Output Control (S/4HANA Public Cloud)
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

 We are using SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and trying to download the XML data for a Purchase Contract using the Manage Purchase Contracts app.

Following SAP documentation/notes, we attempted to change the Output Details in Output Control → Items so that we can enter the code:

 
#&&DOWNLOAD_FORM_DATA_XML&&#

in the Print Queue to download the XML form data.

However, the Output Details fields are greyed out and cannot be edited.

What we have checked so far:

  • Created new purchase contract documents to ensure it is not old data

  • Verified the Output Status is "In Preparation"

  • Checked that the output item exists in Output Control

  • Despite this, Output Details remains non-editable

Because of this, we cannot insert the required code #&&DOWNLOAD_FORM_DATA_XML&&# into the print queue to download the XML.

Question

Has anyone encountered this issue before?
Is there any configuration, authorization, or output parameter setting that could cause the Output Details to be greyed out in the Manage Purchase Contracts app?

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Best practice: SAP Ariba Analytical Reporting API
entry.content:
API modes at a glance

  • Synchronous
    • Paginated: up to 50 records per page.
    • Max 300 pages/day → 50 × 300 = 15,000 records/day.
    • Provides a /count endpoint that returns the total number of results for a view template + filters.
    • Best for very small, ad-hoc queries (few fields, narrow date ranges).
  • Asynchronous
    • Output delivered in ZIP files: 5,000 records per file.
    • Up to 10 files per job → 50,000 records/job.
    • Files expire 48 hours after creation.
    • Up to 40 async jobs/day → theoretical max ≈ 2,000,000 records/day.
    • Jobs are also paginated, meaning if the count returns 80,000 records, 2nd jobs with same filters can be submitted for the 30,000 records. (1st job capped at 50,000 records).
    • Best for bulk exports and scheduled ETL.

Rate limits and important behavior

  • Each API response includes rate-limit headers showing remaining rate-limit per second, minute, hour, and day. Measure and log these values so clients can adapt dynamically. You will never hit 429 error by comparing the last time call was made, what is remain, and when you can start calling again.
  • If a short-window limit (e.g., per-minute) is exceeded you’ll receive HTTP 429. A 429 response still counts as one call toward larger windows (hour/day). Repeated 429s therefore consume broader quotas quickly.
  • Design retry logic with exponential backoff and respect rate-limit headers; avoid tight retry loops after 429s.

Use /count intelligently

  • Call the /count end point (with the same view template and filters) to learn result size before paging.
  • If the /count end point indicates many records, switch to asynchronous export or narrow the filters. This prevents your synchronous page/day quota from being exhausted unnecessarily.

Why synchronous can be problematic in shared/on-demand scenarios

  • Synchronous paging is limited and expensive in aggregate. If multiple users repeatedly run on-demand synchronous extracts for similar datasets, they will quickly deplete shared rate limits (per-second/minute/hour/day), causing 429s and impacting other consumers.
  • Use a central ETL or async exports for shared or repeated large queries.

Concise best practices

  • Pick the right mode: synchronous for tiny interactive checks; asynchronous for bulk loads or scheduled ETL.
  • Use the /count end point first to decide the mode.
  • Minimize payloads: only select required fields and use tight filters or incremental patterns.
  • Monitor and log rate-limit headers (remaining per second/minute/hour/day).
  • Retry policy: exponential backoff with capped attempts; after 429, back off and re-check rate limit remaining in headers.
  • Automate immediate downloads; async files expire after 48 hours.
  • Orchestrate jobs to stagger jobs and respect the 40-job/day cap.

Example workflows 

  • Interactive UI check (synchronous): call the /count end point → if count small, fetch pages and display; else prompt background async export.
  • Nightly datawarehouse sync (asynchronous): schedule job for daily deltas → poll until complete → download ZIPs → ingest within 48 hours.

Use the /count end point to avoid costly synchronous paging, prefer asynchronous jobs for bulk ETL, and always measure/adapt to the per-second/minute/hour/day limits returned in responses. Avoid letting many users run on-demand synchronous extracts against the same data; centralize extraction or use async exports to preserve rate limit.

Extras: For Operational reporting for Procurement or Sourcing, the same practice should be followed. The only difference is the number of jobs and the maximum records per file for Asynchronous call/mode.

Operational Reporting — Procurement vs Sourcing

  • Asynchronous outputs use smaller files: 1,000 records per file.
  • Each job can produce up to 10 files → max 10,000 records/job.
  • Daily job limits differ:
    • Procurement: 40 async jobs/day → theoretical max 400,000 records/day (40 × 10,000).
    • Sourcing: 150 async jobs/day → theoretical max 1,500,000 records/day (150 × 10,000).

entry.title: Why Compound Employee Delta Integrations May Behave Differently After ONB 2.0 Migration
entry.content:
Introduction

The move from ONB 1.0 to ONB 2.0 results in a major architectural shift in the underlying data model of Onboarding. This change has downstream impacts even on existing integrations consuming Employee Central data. Many organisations assume that since Employee Central itself is not being reconfigured, any integrations to and from it are safe. However, as we will see, this assumption can sometimes lead to unexpected failures.

This blog describes how an organisation’s long-standing payroll integration pulling employee master data from EC failed after migration to ONB 2.0, even though it had no direct dependency on the Onboarding module.

Integration Failure After ONB 2.0 Migration

A customer’s go-live after migration to ONB 2.0 appeared smooth. However, one of their Payroll integrations failed in the first few runs post-migration. The integration was designed to retrieve new hire data from Employee Central via the Compound Employee API, process it and send to their third-party Payroll system.

The error message at the XSLT mapping step indicated that the mapping expected a single National ID Card record to be present in the payload. This error resulted from there being two records instead.

If this were a possibility, then why did the integration never fail before? Why now, after so many years?

The reason lies in an important architectural change introduced with the move to ONB 2.0 from ONB 1.0. In ONB 2.0, onboarding now uses the same underlying data model as Employee Central. This also gives rise to a well-known issue where queries to EC entities now begin returning data for onboarding candidates in addition to hired employees. The SAP KBA 3017752 explains that this can be resolved by including the hiringNotCompleted field in the query filter set to false so that the Compound Employee API returns only data of hired employees.

Our issue, however, had a different cause.

Why the Payload Behaviour Changed

The delta integration in question processed new hires (those hired since the previous day’s integration run) and sent their details to the Payroll system. Since the new hire data was processed within a day of the hire, it was reasonable to assume that the data would not be modified in any way within this short span.

In payload terms, this meant that the Compound Employee response would contain only one record per portlet, one with Action=INSERT. This was apparently a reasonable assumption since it had been working reliably for years.

This behaviour changed with the move to ONB 2.0 as data for new hires was now created way earlier in EC under ONB 2.0 as compared to ONB 1.0. In ONB 2.0, onboarding candidates are assigned a user ID and their data is mapped to and populated in the core EC entities (PerPersonal, EmpEmployment, JobInformation etc.) as soon as onboarding is initiated. Because this data is written to EC when onboarding begins rather than when the candidate is finally hired (after completion of Manage Pending Recruits step), there is now a longer time window during which the candidate’s data may be modified. Addresses may be corrected, HR may format the contact information, Job Information may be modified, and National ID may be deleted and a different one created. 

Since the data lifecycle from EC’s perspective now begins way before the actual hiring (at Onboarding initiation to be exact), all this history of changes will be reflected in the Compound Employee payload. This means that the delta integration may now receive multiple records per portlet with a mix of INSERT, CHANGE and DELETE action types depending on how the data was modified since Onboarding triggered. Previously, the integration did not have to iterate through multiple records per portlet and neither had to deal with nested “Previous” nodes which may now be present in the payload.

Conclusion

Ultimately, it all depends on how the payload from Compound Employee API is processed. If the integration has already been designed to accommodate the presence of multiple records with different Action types, then all is well.

However, the point to keep in mind is that migration to ONB 2.0 requires thorough testing prior to going live. Just because it is ‘only’ the Onboarding module that is changing and EC has not been reconfigured, it is tempting to assume that EC related integrations will not be impacted. Practically, the data lifecycle within EC has fundamentally shifted.

Integrations must be reviewed and, if required, adapted to handle multiple action types per portlet, nested ‘previous’ nodes and the presence of hiringNotCompleted users in the incoming payloads. Testing with realistic ONB 2.0 scenarios is essential, particularly for payroll related flows where integration downtimes are high-risk.
